summary.intent: Fix a failing CI pipeline for the moltis project, then add a pre-release workflow capability to allow publishing builds for QA without triggering auto-update notifications for end users.
summary.outcome: Fixed two sequential CI failures (invalid apt package name, then outdated Vulkan SDK version), and added a `pre_release` workflow_dispatch input to release.yml that builds and publishes artifacts as a GitHub prerelease while skipping Homebrew tap updates, deploy template tags, and releases.json updates.

repo: Auto-update notifications are driven by website/releases.json being updated via the `update-deploy-template-tags` job — users poll https://www.moltis.org/releases.json hourly.
The release workflow has three notification-path jobs to skip for silent releases: `update-homebrew-tap`, `update-deploy-template-tags`, and the releases.json deploy step.
LunarG's Ubuntu 22.04 (jammy) apt repo does not have a standalone `glslc` or `shaderc` package — `glslang-tools` is the correct package name, and `vulkan-sdk` is the meta-package for the full SDK.
llama-cpp-sys-2 v0.1.133 uses Vulkan C++ API features (LayerSettingsCreateInfoEXT, eMesaDozen) requiring Vulkan SDK >= ~1.3.275; Ubuntu 22.04's base repos only provide libvulkan-dev 1.3.204.
Commit 681e9f5b introduced the broken `glslc` package name that caused the initial CI failure.

code.finding: LunarG repo setup must occur before libvulkan-dev install; replaced `libvulkan-dev` (Ubuntu base, v1.3.204) + `glslang-tools` with `vulkan-sdk` from LunarG to get up-to-date Vulkan headers.
Added `pre_release` boolean workflow_dispatch input; when true, GitHub release is marked prerelease and `update-homebrew-tap` + `update-deploy-template-tags` jobs are skipped via `if` conditions.
Update check logic lives here — polls releases.json from the website to determine if a newer version is available.
Stable channel install manifest — separate from releases.json used for update notifications.

workflow: When CI fails with a package-not-found error, verify the exact package name against the actual repo's package index (e.g., fetching the Packages file directly) rather than assuming the package name.
Sequential CI failures on the same PR are common when fixing one issue exposes a deeper pre-existing one — always check if the new failure is related to the same change.

GitHub workflow_dispatch with a `dry_run` vs `pre_release` distinction is a useful pattern: dry_run skips everything including builds, pre_release builds and publishes but suppresses user-facing notifications.

friction: Two separate CI failures required two separate fix-commit-push cycles, with the second failure only revealed after the first fix landed.
A git push conflict required a pull-rebase before pushing on both fix commits.

The user initially triggered a dry_run workflow instead of the new pre_release workflow, causing confusion about why Docker builds were skipped — the pre_release feature didn't exist yet at that point.
GitHub security hook flagged workflow file edits, requiring retries.
open_items: Once QA passes on the pre-release build, the user needs to manually unmark the GitHub release as prerelease and trigger or run the deploy-tags/homebrew update steps to notify users.
The dry run workflow (run 23498472801) is still open/in-progress from before the pre_release feature — the user should verify it can be ignored.
docs/src/local-validation.md was updated to match the glslang-tools change but should be reviewed to ensure accuracy for local dev setup instructions.
